chunks.text: All right, everybody, Greg Koukl here. I'm Stand to Reason, and I am really looking forward to this next hour with you and my guest. He's actually, I think, just about the only guest I've ever had on for two whole hours. He's been that much fun for me in the past to interview. We are only doing an hour right now. He's very, very busy, and we'll be talking about why he's very busy. He is the author of Signature in the Cell, which I devoured, Darwin's Doubt, which I devoured, and Return of the God Hypothesis, which I also devoured and really leaned on heavily in my last book, The Street Smarts. And of course, you'll know him as the director of Discovery Institute Center for Science and Culture in Seattle. And of course, I'm talking about Stephen C. Meyer. Stephen, what a treat to talk to you again on the air. It's been a few years since your last book in our last interview, but it's great to have you on board. I'm delighted to connect. It's always fun to talk, whether on air or off air. Yeah, I remember a year or so ago, I was somewhere in Wisconsin about three and a half hours from our place in northern Wisconsin. I was driving back by myself and I thought, I just got to listen to something interesting. And I just did a whole bunch of YouTube interviews that you were doing. And you had so many of them dealing with the return of the God hypothesis. I was enjoying it so much I had to text you. And then we carried on a text conversation, didn't we? And you were in Cambridge at the time, getting a new center together. How's that going? It's going extremely well that we were able to purchase two properties for Discovery Institute and a new, for Discovery to found a new institute called the William Hewel Center for Science and Natural Theology, named for a very important 19th century philosopher of science, scientist, and divine. He was a theologian as well. And the center is devoted to reviving and to studying the rich tradition of British natural theology that started with figures like Isaac Newton, one of his mentors, Isaac Barrow. One of Isaac Barrow's mentors, John Ray. And other figures like Robert Boyle, Johannes Kepler. And so Kepler not being British, but the rest being British scientists who, I believe that when they studied nature, they were seeing the handiwork of God and they took inspiration from the passage in Romans 1 that says, From the creation of the world, God's invisible qualities, his eternal power and divine nature or wisdom has been clearly being understood from what has been made. It was the study of those things that had been made that they believed led them to an awareness of the reality of God. Well, that's basically what we want to talk about today because incidentally, I think Amy's going to be out there in August with you guys in Cambridge. Joining our course, I believe. Yeah. And I looked at my own calendar and I thought, man, is there any way I get to get shoehorned into this program for a couple of weeks there? Turns out I have events I can't sneak by. I thought maybe I can get the permission of the director, you know, to my wife and I to get out there. It's a good excuse to get to England and, you know, just be there with you guys. But that's not going to work out. Amy's going to be there, though. And I think the Kunkels late of Stand to Reason, Brett and Erin Kunkel, are going to be there, too. So they're going to have a great time without me. But you'll be there. I presume you'll be part of that program. Is that right? Yeah. I'm kind of the lead teacher. And the course is based on a course I taught for 15 years as a college professor, which I used to call Reasons for Faith. This time I'm calling it From Cosmos to Christ. And we're investigating the evidential and philosophical basis of the Christian faith. So it's more of an apologetics course and something that's strictly focused on intelligent design. Yeah. Well, the occasion here for our conversation is a new film that you have that's being released. It's a documentary that you're a principal player in. And there are a lot of other fabulous people that are being interviewed. But, I mean, you have you it seems to me you've been everywhere in the last two years since it's been about a year and a half since Rogan, your time with Rogan. And then you've been on Piers Morgan. And every time I turn around, I see another clip with you with some other big YouTuber, you know. But you're doing such a great job, Stephen. And we've known each other even before Stand to Reason started. You know, I'm thinking way, way, way back. And it's been great to watch how God has used everything that you've done. And in particular, this corner you turned with the return of the God hypothesis, because the corner there, now it's not just intelligent design. You're actually, in a certain sense, identifying the designer as God. And that's a philosophical shift. And now it's taking roots now in this documentary that will have a national release later in the month of April, I think April 30th to May 6th, right? The Story of Everything. Yeah, that's right. We have a guaranteed seven night opening. Depending on how the film does, we may kick into a second week. We'll see. And then we've already signed a nice contract for a digital release on multiple platforms. So you'll be able to find it. But the theatrical release is exciting because there's a possibility of kind of creating a national buzz around this mission. I've got an op-ed coming out tomorrow morning with Fox Digital talking about the scientific evidence for the existence of God, pegging it to some of the observations that the astronauts have made. And it's so frequent when the astronauts go into space, they have an almost spiritual or religious experience. You could go back to the Christmas Day reading of the book of Genesis from the Apollo 8 astronauts. You and I are old enough to remember because people today were born after the last time the NASA people were on the moon. But the current head of NASA, Jared Isaacman, has said that his time in space convinced him of the truth of the biblical phrase, the heavens declare the glory of God. And what current astronauts on this mission have said similar things. So there's just an experience that so many astronauts have when they look back at that blue jewel of planet Earth against the blackness and the depth of the rest of space. And it does kind of give you a sense that something very special happened here and maybe there was a hidden hand behind it all. Yeah, well, it's so ironic you bring that particular pale blue dot image up. That was obviously the book written by Carl Sagan before he died 25 years ago or whatever it was. But he did not see it that way. He saw the pale blue dot that was evidenced by what spaceship was leaving the solar system or whatever and kind of took a look over the shoulder and took that famous picture. That was the Voyager or something. Yeah, something going out to Pluto, I think. Yeah, yeah. And so anyway, but it was like, and his conclusion was, we are nothing because we are so small and therefore inconsequential. And it struck me as odd that he would draw this conclusion about humankind based on the size of the universe and by comparison, the smallness of human beings, that he would just assume that smallness means loss of value or no value at all. It's just so odd. And then your astronauts are drawing an opposite kind of conclusion. Sometimes almost intuitively, but there is a logical basis for the inference in the other direction. And that's that the number of conditions that need to be met, that need to be in place, and the improbability associated with each of them and collective improbability on the ensemble has led increasing numbers of astronomers to jettison the idea that life in the universe is inevitable and instead to see our planet as something incredibly special. There's a book, of course, our colleagues Jay Richards and Guillermo Gonzalez wrote the famous book, now famous book, Privileged Planet. But even before them, two secular astronomers and physicists at the University of Washington wrote the famous book called Rare Earth. And now there's an Italian group of scientists that have been coming to the same conclusion. But the key issue there is the fine tuning of those parameters. And that you have this incredible improbability that's necessary to produce life. And as Jay and Guillermo have pointed out, not only that same set of conditions that's necessary to allow for life on the planet is the exact same set of conditions that makes it possible to learn about the universe, to make scientific discoveries. So we touch on that in the film. I think what you're hitting on is that there's basically two stories of science, two interpretations. And we've only heard one. We always hear the interpretation that we're a pale blue dot, or as in the film, we have a nice clip from Sagan where he says, here we are, like mites on a plum, on this little tiny obscure planet in the outskirts of an

insignificant galaxy, one of about 400 billion other galaxies. And we show this flyby as you go out into space. Very impressive. Kind of vertigo feeling like, well, maybe we are very insignificant. So on the one hand, you have, we're very small in relation to the vastness of the universe, but the conditions that made our lives possible, that made any life possible, are extremely specific and would have required a great deal of intentionality and forethought indeed. And so that's the tension. The film develops those two stories, but then shows that the evidence, I think, is overwhelmingly, overwhelmingly in favor of the theistic story, not the nihilistic materialistic story. You know, I finished watching it last night. My wife and I took two evenings to get half of it, and then the next. It's a little shy of two hours. And it's so absolutely fascinating, so gripping to watch. And this is stuff, I knew half of the people, well, not half, but a lot of the people that were being interviewed, you, Jay, a bunch of them. And I was familiar with the basic arc of the story. Nevertheless, I was once again just completely captivated by the power of this material. And your promotional material says it's a science-first examination of life's biggest questions. And at somewhere along the line during the film, somebody, some astronomer is saying, I don't know if it's Alan Sandage or not Hoyle, but somebody, and they're saying, I'm looking at this evidence, but I don't want to give it a theological interpretation. Somewhere in there they mention. And what occurred to me is, you don't have to, like, leave reality and go to theology. A theological assessment is what the reality demonstrates is the most appropriate. And that's what's so wonderful about the way this all unfolds here in this film, The Story of Everything, and, of course, trading a lot on your material in Return of the God Hypothesis. Actually, also Signature in the Cell. The content of both of those books is featured here significantly. Well, and I've been asked a lot, Greg, is this a faith-based documentary or a faith-based film? And my answer is no. It's a science-based film that is faith-affirming. And in another interview I did today with a somewhat more hostile interviewer, he said, Don't you worry? Didn't you ever worry that you were going to cross over from science into theology? I said, Well, why would that be a worry? Wouldn't it be very exciting to learn that scientific evidence was pointing to the reality of God? Wouldn't that be one of the most exciting things you could ever learn? And we've had this idea that we have to keep knowledge in these separate boxes. But the exciting thing, I think, is that the discoveries that we're looking at concerning the origin of the universe, the origin of the fine-tuning of the universe, and the origin of life itself, are subjects that are both scientific, but they have larger metaphysical implications. And so today, students have only been given one interpretation of the evidence, and that's the materialistic one. But when you open up the discussion and allow yourself to consider the possibility behind the physical evidence that we see in biology, physics, and cosmology, the best explanation is actually that there was a mind behind all this. And that's a very exciting thing to consider, and in the film, we weigh those two competing stories of reality. Yeah, and you do that so well in the film, all of this ensemble of intellects, and including statements from those who don't hold the view and others that, as you demonstrate in the book and you characterize, we'll get into some of this detail, were kind of dragged kicking and screaming against their will into a theistic view of the universe because they simply could not continue to argue against the implications of the evidence. I'm thinking right now about the origin of the universe and that whole thing, but that also continues. Right, yeah, and you referred to that a minute ago, and I think the person you were talking about, well, there were two. One was Alan Sandage, who was a great astronomer, astrophysicist, cosmologist who was at Caltech and at the Mount Wilson Observatory. And the other was Robert Jastrow, both very important figures in the 1980s. And Jastrow wrote a famous book called God and the Astronomers. And he was officially still agnostic, but he essentially acknowledged that the evidence was pointing to theism, to a theistic creator. And we have a clip from him in the film where he's being asked by Hugh Downs, one of the journalists at the time. I remember Hugh Downs. I'm old enough. And he says, is there another way to interpret this evidence? Is there a way of getting around the theological implications or explanation? And Jastrow pauses for a long time, and he starts to say something, and he says, No, he says, I just can't see it. You know, he's torn, and you can see that internal struggle. And Sandage's story was very similar. And I saw, I witnessed a very powerful talk he gave at a conference early in my career when he did the exposition. He explained the scientific evidence for the beginning of the universe, for its fine-tuning. And then he looked into the camera and said, Here is evidence for what can only be described as a supernatural event. There's no way this could have been predicted or explained within the realm of physics as we know it. You can't explain the origin of the physical universe by reference to physical laws. There were no physical laws before you had physics and before you had a physical system. And Sandage struggled and struggled with this. He had been a longtime agnostic and a hard-bitten scientific materialist. And he eventually realized that there was something in him that he was uncomfortable with, that he didn't want it to be true. He knew where the evidence was pointing, but he didn't want it to be true. And then he said he had to do some soul-searching because he had to ask. He prided himself on his scientific objectivity. But about this one question, he was not capable of being objective. His desire for it not to be true was outweighing what the evidence was affirming. And he eventually did, and now he announced at that conference a religious conversion. He became a Christian eventually, and it was a really interesting thing to witness. We were able to go back and find that footage of what I saw when I was 27 years old that rocked my world and was one of the factors that led to my deep interest in both the origin of the universe and the origin of life as parts of my life's work. Yeah, I've heard you tell about the account of Sandage a number of times, and people can get all this stuff. Just type in Stephen C. Meyer on your YouTube and you're going to get tons of interviews where Stephen goes over a lot of these details. It's fascinating. And I've listened to them many, many times. But as I recall, no one was expecting him to say that in that situation. On the front, it was divided between theists and materialists or atheist agnostics, and each of the panels discussing these big questions. And the origin of the universe panel was divided between theists and materialists. Sandage ascended to the podium and sat down surprisingly to everyone in the audience with the other theists and then what I call the God hypothesis. And it was quite a shocking kind of thing for a lot of people to see that. But then he gave a brilliant explanation as to why he had changed his worldview or metaphysical hypothesis of reality. And one thing is, we get to tell that story in the film and have some of the primary footage from even that singular event. You know, this story of everything, when I saw that as the title of the documentary that will be released nationally April 30th through May 6th, so there's a whole week there for sure. And then we'll see how the numbers work. And just so everybody knows in advance, thestoryofeverything.film is the URL to get more information about that, thestoryofeverything.film. I never saw a .film before, but I guess they're running out of .coms on all the others. But you know what I thought of when I saw the title is I thought of Stephen Hawking's The Theory of Everything, his life's pursuit, trying to find a way to bring all of reality under kind of one heading. And I don't understand, my wife and I had the same reaction when we finished the film last night, this one, The Story of Everything, we had an advanced viewing of it. And how is it that even the fiercest critic can't see that this is the unifying principle of the universe, God Himself? And it solves all the problems, not just in scientific but existential as well. Yeah, I had a very interesting conversation years ago, several years ago, with Thomas Nagel, who got himself into a bit of trouble with fellow atheists after he misbehaved. Yeah, well, he made the mistake first of commending my book with the Times Literary Supplement in London. And

And then later doubled down and wrote his own book, Mind in Cosmos, How the Neo-Darwinian Materialist Account of Reality is Almost Certainly False was the subtitle. And he agreed to meet me, and we had a long lunch in New York, and he's a wonderful man, a very deep, deep-thinking philosopher, and still an atheist or agnostic. And he was asking me about how I had become a Christian and what were the philosophical considerations. And I started to explain that one of the arguments that really convinced me of theism in the first place, of the existence of God, was the argument from epistemological necessity, the idea that a belief in God grounds our belief in the reliability of the mind and thus makes knowledge possible. And I started to explain the argument, and he cut me off, and he said, no, no, no, you don't need to explain this to me. He said, there's no question that theism solves a lot of philosophical problems. And he and Alvin Plantinga have been, and I think Nagel is still with us, and he and Plantinga have been friends for a very long time, and Plantinga being the great theistic philosopher, Nagel being the atheistic philosopher, and they have tremendous respect for each other. And Nagel is famous for his candor about the God question, saying that he just simply does not want the universe to be the kind of place in which there is a person such as God. And I think, you know, I think you find a figure like Hawking, he wasn't as explicit about that, but so much of his work pointed to a definite beginning of the universe, and then he spent much of the rest of his life trying to find a kind of loophole to circumvent that conclusion with his ideas about what's called quantum cosmology, which we touch on briefly, but not deeply in the film. I go into a great deal and a great deal of depth on it in the book, but we didn't want to... Well, I think that was helpful when you brought that issue up with Hawking because what it shows is how hard, how vigorously many of these people struggle to deny the obvious. And this may seem to people listening like it's self-serving. If you watch The Story of Everything, go see it. You will see that the theistic solution to unifying all knowledge and all human reality and everything makes so much sense, and the evidence for it is so compelling. You develop in your book, Return of the God Hypothesis, which I strongly recommend. It's a long read, obviously, but it's so much fun, and it's so rich, Stephen, with the way you handle all the material and you integrated your years and years of experience, not only learning these things, but communicating them effectively to audiences. So it's a very readable book. You don't have to be a scientist. But what it shows is kind of what's obvious, and I think this is something that Doug Axe makes obvious in his own work in Undeniable, that we have a design intuition. We can look at things, and we can realize this, it takes know-how to do this. Doug's in the movie as well. He does a short. Doug does a great job in the movie, yeah. He makes a... Yeah, some fabulous expert. So you've got to have the know-how, but this becomes... So it's the, in a certain sense, the gross scientific evidence and also the intuitive element that comes to bear on how is it that you have Lawrence Krauss and Neil deGrasse Tyson and Richard Dawkins and a whole bunch of these people that are so dismissive, almost grossly dismissive. You know, Tyson says in the movie, he said, well, if we look at the evidence, we find a bearded man behind the scenes. Okay, I can live with that. But there's no evidence. What? What's going on there? Yeah, well, in a way, what is going on is we're reaching the tail end of the influence of the new atheists. They were hugely popular and a big force in culture around 07, 08, 09. By 2015, they started at 14, 15. There was some tremendous infighting in the group, and I think they've massively overplayed their hand. And they did not really engage these scientific arguments. There was one new atheist called Victor Stenger that had a book called God, The Failed Hypothesis. And obviously, I begged to differ, and I think they were not engaging the strongest forms of the cosmological argument. They were really taking the fine-tuning argument seriously, and many of them proposed the multiverse as an alternative. And as we show in the film and as I show in more depth in the book, every credible form of multiverse hypothesis requires prior unexplained fine-tuning. So it's related to explain the incredible improbability associated with all the many physical parameters that are necessary to make life possible, the so-called fine-tuning of the universe. And then to explain that improbability, the go-to atheistic argument now, or hypothesis, is to propose billions of other universes, thinking that that would render the probability of a life-friendly universe somewhere much higher. But it turns out that in order to render that hypothesis plausible, you have to have some kind of underlying universe-generating mechanism so that you can portray our universe as the lucky winner of a great cosmic lottery. And then if you examine those universe-generating mechanisms, they're basically competing or different ideas about different cosmological models, and each one of them requires its own prior fine-tuning to generate new universes. So you're right back to where you started. I know. It's not a very compelling explanation. And then Dawkins hitched his wagon to the idea that Darwin made it possible to be an intellectually fulfilled atheist. And as of 10 years ago, you had many leading evolutionary biologists starting to call for a new theory of evolution because they knew that the Darwinian mechanism of mutation and selection lacks the creative power to generate fundamentally new forms of life we see arising in the history of life. So on each of these key core topics, they really didn't have very strong scientific argument, and yet they claimed that science made belief in God untenable. Yeah. I think it was, I saw something with Lawrence Krauss, who had an abysmal debate with William Lane Craig, abysmal in the sense that he was a very, what's the right word? Unsavory debater. Yeah. Well, he resorts to ad hominem abuse so quickly, and audiences see through that. I think that's one of the reasons the new atheism has failed. Yeah. Anyway, he made a statement, everybody should be an atheist if they're going to be reasonable human beings. And it was just, that, I think, was completely self-serving. Incidentally, I want to go back just a moment to the multiverse issue because I love the way you develop and expose the liabilities of that view. But the reason I want to go back to it is because it's so popular. The design argument, the fine-tuned constants of the universe, which you go into great detail to describe, is so powerful and compelling. I think even Richard Dawkins says this is like the hardest, the most difficult. And his response to the whole thing is, well, we just got lucky, kind of thing. And maybe he advances multiverse. But you change your wording there at one point. You said, well, the argument but the hypothesis. In other words, you talked about the hypothesis of the multiverse, not the argument, which I think was appropriate because there's no argument there. It is an alternate explanation, but the evidence that such a thing is plausible or likely, that's missing as far as I've been able to tell. Is that fair? Well, yeah, you could say there's an argument in support of the hypothesis, and the argument is that if all these universes exist, then one in some universe, then the probability shift, and it becomes more probable that in some universe you will have a set of life-friendly physical conditions. But the problem with that is that if these universes are actually separate universes, if they're causally distinct from one another, it's the whole idea of the multiverse, then anything that happens in some other universe that is not causally connected to ours, that has no causal effect on ours, has no effect on anything in our universe, including whatever process set the fine-tuning parameters in the first place. And so simply positing other universes by itself does nothing to shift the probabilities in favor of saying that somewhere a life-friendly universe must arise or that the, or does nothing to explain the improbability of the fine-tuning in our universe. So in virtue of that, what the fine-tuning, what the multiverse proponents have to do is posit some sort of underlying universe-generating mechanism or process. And there are two that have been proposed. One is based on string theory and the other is based on inflationary cosmology. And it turns out in both cases that for those, the processes envisioned in those speculative cosmologies to produce,

these new universes, the the processes themselves have to be finely tuned. And so you're right back to where you started. And in the film we illustrated this with the idea of monkeys at the typewriter. I love this illustration. I love it. And you say, yeah, if you got all the keys on the typewriter, then eventually maybe the monkeys will type Hamlet. But if you don't, if you don't have the letter H on the keyboard, you know, all bets are off. You're never going to get there. And that's a way of showing that the keyboard has to be finely tuned to produce something even by chance that you want. Now I'd heard about the monkeys and Hamlet before, but never the keyboard with the H. And that adds a whole additional layer. That's so clear to me. It was really wonderful. My wife and I both laughed at that one. The complaint that I have with the so-called argument versus the hypothesis is you said, well, there's an argument if you have multiverses, then it has explanatory power for the fine-tuning of this universe. At least that's the way they argue. But that's a huge if. And so what I've been looking for is, all right, what are the, what is the evidence that you have that such a thing is actually the case here? And that's why there's no argument on my view because it's a hypothesis that saves the paradigm, the materialistic paradigm, but there's no evidence to think that that's actually the way it happened. Am I missing something in that? Well, it may be, I think it's fair game to postulate something on the basis of its possible explanatory power. But what the multiverse in the cosmological context is postulating, has been postulated to explain, is the fine-tuning. Now, there are some physics justifications for positing it that come out of quantum mechanics. But the point is that even if it's true, even if there is a multiverse, you haven't explained fine-tuning, you've only pushed it out of view. You've just pushed it back one generation. So, I mean, I think it's extremely implausible. There's a lot, it's a, obviously it's a huge stretch. Yeah. But it's fair game. You know, people could say the same thing about the God hypothesis. Well, there's no direct evidence for God. Well, true, but when you posit God or an entity that has the attributes that the theists attribute to God, transcendence, intelligence, the ability to be active in the creation, that postulation has vastly superior explanatory power when compared with other competing metaphysical systems. So, that's the kind of the inference to the best explanation approach, which is great. But I guess I push back a little bit on their claim that there's no direct evidence for God. Unless they mean we don't have a photograph for God. But if we have, we never see our mailman, but the mail shows up every single day, you know, I mean, that's evidence, that's tangible, concrete evidence. And this is the thing I think that many material, just ordinary people, the strongest reason for not believing in God is just that we can't see him. But we believe in so many scientific realities or entities or postulates because of their explanatory power. And when the physicists proposed dark matter, they still haven't seen dark matter, but they proposed it because only the postulation of dark matter could explain certain gravitational anomalies in the things that we saw in outer space. We don't see quarks, we don't see subatomic particles, we don't see subterranean geological faults, we don't even see the structure of the DNA molecule. All these things are inferred as because of their ability to explain things we can't see. That's just the structure of scientific inference and reasoning. And if that's a legitimate way to reason in the scientific realm, then why is that not a legitimate way to reason when we're talking about an inference to a designing intelligence or to God himself? Chesterton once said, you know, I don't believe in God because I see him, but I believe in him because by him I see all other things. It's the explanatory power of the theistic hypothesis that commends it to the intellect. So this reminds me just of forensic pathology, because what you have there is, let's say you have a body that you find, all right, and a corpse. Now they have to ask, answer the question, does this person die from natural causes or foul play? Was it event causation or was there agency involved? How do they determine that? They look at the nature of the evidence. If there are bullet holes in the head and a knife in the back and all those other things, well, that's suspicious, right? And bloody footprints leaving the room. There you go. Yeah, yeah. So I don't know why it's so, they're so completely comfortable with that, yet they cry foul when we make the same kind of legitimate inferences from the evidence we see. Even in archaeology, for goodness sake, you've got a piece of stone that's been struck a certain way is evidence for the intelligent design of that stone, even though you don't know who the individual was that struck the stone. Well, absolutely, Greg. And my whole PhD was about historical or forensic reasoning and the way it's used in the historical and evolutionary sciences, particularly in origin of life research, which is the focus of my work. But a really, an example that I've been using that really hits home with people right away, having a discussion in an Uber car and people want to know, well, how, why do you believe in intelligent designer? Or if someone said, well, the reason I'm, I'd like to believe in God, but I, I don't think I can because I can't see God. And I said, do you believe in Steve Jobs? And they say, what do you mean? Pulls up the iPhone. Can you explain the origin of the iPhone without postulating the existence of Steve Jobs? He's no longer with us. He's dead. We don't see him, but we know that there was a mind behind the creation of the iPhone and any, any system that stores digital information or has the capacity to produce digital information, we know instinctively because of our knowledge of the cause and effect structure of the world, that this system was produced by a designing mind. And lo and behold, that's what we find inside living cells is not only digital information stored in the DNA molecule, but a whole information storage transmission and processing system that not only rivals, but exceeds the processing systems that we have in our own high-tech digital technology. So we can move confidently from observable effects back to unobservable causes if we know something about the cause and effect structure of the world. Something I learned that I hadn't heard of before in the film I saw last night was that the digital code, which is actually kind of an alpha, alphabetical, alphanumeric. No. What do you call it? Just alphabetical code. This was correct sequence hypothesis. Sequence hypothesis. But that can be, it can be two things that I didn't know. First of all, it can be converted to zeros and ones. That they talk about. And then, and also it can be read forward and backwards and it gives us a different set of directions when forward as opposed to backwards. Yeah. There are a few proteins where it can go both ways. And then there's a whole bunch of overlapping genes that produce different proteins on the same strand by simply shifting the reading reference frame. So, and that's just, that's just stunning because it's, it's hard enough to imagine how random mutations, natural selection acting random mutations could generate a new set of instructions for a new protein at all. And I could go into that why that's the case. But when you think about random changes having to satisfy multiple semantic requirements simultaneously, this just, this is crazy making. I mean, it's not, it's not plausible at all. You know, you were part of the very first film I've seen on, on this issue of the, the DNA and the little machines, the nanotechnology inside the cell, which is amazing. And it was called, is it unlocking the mystery of life? Unlocking the mystery of life 2003. Yeah. We released it on the 50th anniversary of the publication of the Watson and Crick paper in 1953. On April 25th, 2003. We had a nice run with that. It got onto PBS and of course the graphics and animations that we can render now that really show what's going on are, they were great then, but they're even better now. Yeah. Well, that's the point I was going to make. That was great. And I, I had like many OMG moments when I'm, you know, Oh my golly, right? My moments while I'm watching this because it was so incredible. Of course, what you've done in the story of everything is, is like the difference between Flash Gordon and, you know, Star Wars or something like that. Of course, maybe nobody knows who Flash Gordon was. The special effects. You could actually see the strings hanging from the rocket ships. What you've done in the story of everything in terms of rendering these, this nanotechnology and the way inside the cell, you have all of these machines, building machines, building machines, and they're all running the cell. That is awesome. Just awesome to watch it. The producers did a fantastic job. There are 400 visual effects in the film and the, and the cinematography of deep space is gorgeous. It really, and, and it was really designed with a big screen in mind. So it pops. I saw it on a big screen for the first time a week ago, Saturday, at a screening in Nashville. And I've been, you know, giving feedback to the director on my, from watching things on my iPhone and to see it on the big screen. It just, it affected me, you know, Yeah. It's overwhelming. Well

Kind of jumping all around here regarding the story of everything has been a lot of fun, but I kind of want to jump back to the beginning because this is where... this is the beginning, this is the start of everything, the change and the shift from the early 20th century with Einstein and Hubble and the red shift and all these other things that started to change people's thinking that was deeply committed to a steady state universe that never had a beginning, and so they didn't have to deal with the God thing. Even Carl Sagan makes a reference to that in the movie. And then the facts just kept intruding. Maybe you could tell us a little bit about how that story developed and, you know, some of the cheating that Einstein attempted and got caught and was sorry for after it was over, but go ahead. The really fun things about the way the producers put together the story is that we tell the story of the overarching shift to the return of the God hypothesis, the reembrace of the understanding that there must be a transcendent, intelligent mind behind the universe. But also we tell the stories of individual scientists who underwent that shift themselves, and one of them, of course, is Alan Sandage, and we tell his story. There's another story within that story, and it's the story of Einstein. And he develops a new theory of gravity in 1915 called general relativity. The idea of that is that massive bodies actually curve the fabric of space, or what he called space-time. And he realizes immediately, if that's true, and if gravity is the only force acting in the universe, that then everything should have congealed into one giant black hole. The space would have gotten so tightly curved that nothing could have gotten out, even light. And yet we don't live in that kind of universe. We live in a universe in which there's empty space between massive bodies. So he reasons that there must be a corresponding outward pushing force that is creating that empty space. But that implies a dynamic and expanding universe and outward from the beginning. And he said, well, you know, we can't have that because he, like scientists at the turn of the 19th into the 20th century, presupposes that the universe is eternal and self-existent. And we have an eternal, self-existent, self-creating, materialistic universe that does not require an external creator. And so to save that philosophical predilection or assumption, he posits that the outward pushing force called the cosmological constant is of such a strength that it balances exactly the inward pull of gravity. And so he can redepict the universe as in a kind of constant steady state. It's a static universe, not a dynamic expanding universe. And then some of the physicists start to work with these equations and they realize even if you posit this precise value for a finely tuned value of the cosmological constant, even slight perturbations in matter and energy anywhere in the universe would cause the universe to either re-expand or compress. It would go back into a dynamic situation. And then beyond that, the astronomers start to discover that the galaxies in all of the quadrants of the night sky are moving away from this. And this comes from their analysis of the light coming from the distant galaxies and how the wavelengths of light are being stretched out by that recessional velocity. And that's the work first of, sorry, the American astronomer Vesto Slipher. There you go. I was having trouble pulling it. Yeah, I'm not going to be much help for you on that. Amy is my helper on that kind of thing. So go ahead. And then Hubble and his colleague Humason. And Einstein has a memorable interaction with the Belgian Catholic priest who actually is the founder of the Big Bang theory. And Einstein confronts him and says, You know, you're all wrong here. Your mathematics is impeccable, but your physical intuition is abominable. The universe must be static. And a couple of years later, the Cambridge physicist, Arthur Eddington, who didn't like the Big Bang much either, but he confronts Einstein and he says, You got to get out to California and see the evidence. And so he goes out to the Mount Wilson Observatory and we have the newsreel footage. It's awesome. And Einstein goes up into the telescope. It looks through the eyepiece. And then a couple weeks later, he gives, with Hubble in the background with his pipe. Smoking his pipe, right. And then a couple of weeks later, he gives an interview to the New York Times and says, Essentially, the three hardest words in the English language, I was wrong. And Hubble and Humason have shown that the universe is not static and therefore it must have had a beginning. And Einstein later says, This is the greatest blunder of his life. And I misquoted him in the book. I said that he said that it was the greatest blunder of his scientific career. No, he regarded it as the greatest blunder of his entire life. He allowed his philosophical bias to shape his interpretation of the evidence rather than allowing the evidence to speak. So then you have, as this kind of story continues, you have Fred Hoyle, also a major player in this entire field, that was trying to, I guess, try to avoid the implication of the origin of the universe too. But he ended up having a kind of conversion. I'm not sure the theological extent of that, but this was another circumstance with Sandage and Hoyle and Einstein where these guys could not deny the evidence. It was too compelling. Well, Hoyle's famous because he coined the term the Big Bang. I think on the BBC, it came out of his mouth and he meant it to stigmatize the idea of the universe having a beginning or the theory that the universe must have had a beginning that was emerging in cosmology. And the term stuck, what he meant as a pejorative term. And it turned out to be, you know, the popular name. And he continued to, he formulated an alternative called the steady state theory, but he eventually embraced a kind of rudimentary form of theism or certainly intelligent design because of a different, a discovery he made in a different area of physics. And that was the discovery of the fine-tuning of the fundamental laws and constants of physics that were necessary to account for the abundance of carbon and thus the possibility of life. And he was later quoted after making this extraordinary discovery himself. And we tell the story. Again, the producers and some of the young guys that were working on the research in this, they found audio from an eight-track of someone at Caltech telling the story of how Hoyle came out to have his theory about how carbon was created in the universe tested at Caltech. And his prediction was bang on. And when Hoyle realized that there had to be this very precise way that the carbon molecule would resonate to allow it to form from beryllium and helium, he realized that not only was the carbon, the structure of carbon finely tuned, but the gravitational force constant, the electromagnetic force constant, the speed of light, all these other parameters had to be precisely fine-tuned to allow for the production of carbon and for a life-friendly universe. And at that point he realized, he was later quoted as saying, that a common-sense interpretation of facts suggests that a super intellect has monkeyed with physics as well as biology and chemistry and biology in order to make life possible. So he ends up embracing a form of the design hypothesis and changes his mind. Now, did that just lead him to kind of a generalized theism or he's probably not alive anymore, is that right? Yeah, I got to meet him once when I was in my second year, I think, in Cambridge. And we had a long walk back from a talk that he gave to one of the colleges. And I told him what I was working on with developing this argument from the information in DNA to intelligent design. And when I said that, he sort of, he said, oh, he's motioned to me like, come walk with me. He said, this is very provocative. What a great opportunity. Yeah, he said, yeah, he told me there's no question that if we were free to invoke intelligence, we could do a lot better job of explaining the origin of life. So he at one point formulated a panspermia hypothesis, but he definitely saw the need for intelligence to explain both the fine-tuning and the digital code that started in the DNA. So why doesn't it occur to these guys, and even Hawking, not Hawking, but Dawkins, toyed with the panspermia also. In other words, they kick the can down the road to aliens that sprinkled life here. Why doesn't that occur that that's all they're doing is kicking the can down the road? Well, you know, I suppose it might. No less a personage than Francis Crick proposed the panspermia hypothesis in his little book, Life Itself. And he was quoted as saying, you know, so many are the conditions that would have had to have been met to make life, to make it possible for life to rise on Earth, that it must have happened someplace else. Well, actually, the first thing he says is almost a miracle. And then he proposes...

that life was either designed or seeded here by an intelligent agent. He was never really clear. And simply positing an alien to transport life here to Earth doesn't really solve the ultimate problem of the origin of the information needed to build life. So it's, I like to say, it's not just kicking the can down the road, it's kicking the problem out into space without providing an ultimate solution to the big problem, which is, where's the information coming from? Sure, sure. Jay Richards is featured here. I was glad to see him. He looks like Jeremiah Johnson or something. He's got a full beard and the whole deal. There's a backstory there. We had to film these interviews during COVID. And I had a homemade COVID haircut and he had a beard. Anyway. The producers did the best they could to dress us up. No, I actually thought it was very becoming, but it was a shock there. I say, there's Jay, my wife, and he goes, no, it's not. I said, yes, he is behind all that hair is Jay Richards. It's hard for Jay Richards to not look handsome. It's just, it's very hard. You're right. He looked great, I thought. But I met him for the first time back when you guys had the design and its critics conference. You remember that? That was 30-some years ago, maybe. Concordia University. Concordia, yeah, right by Milwaukee, Wisconsin. And somehow I was able to weasel into this professional conference and a wonderful time watching all of you interact so graciously and thoughtfully with everybody. But speaking of Jay, though, that's another element here where he and Guillermo Gonzalez had written this wonderful book, The Privileged Planet. And let's talk just a little bit. We've got about three or four minutes before we have to finish, but talk a little bit about the so-called Goldilocks zone of the planet Earth. And this, by the way, is acknowledged. This phrase, Goldilocks zone, comes from the secular environment. And you can watch secular films talking about this. Go ahead. Well, and publications and peer-reviewed literature. The establishment, there's a very narrow band within the solar system where life would be possible, where you could get a planet that could hold, for example, liquid water or oxygen on it. Because there's so many localized fine-tuning parameters. We have to have the right gravitational mass of a planet to hold the right kind of atmosphere. It has to be rotating around a host sun with the right kind of axial tilt in order to create seasons. You have to have the right kind of magnetic field projected off the planet to protect from harmful solar radiation. The moon. And you've got to have a moon to stabilize tides. And you need outer planets that are large, you know, gravitational masses to draw in the destructive asteroids and such. And so the number of parameters that are just right and the associated improbability with each in our localized solar system are really awe-inspiring. And it's another Goldilocks thing. Not too far, not too tilted this way, not too tilted that way, not too strong, not too weak. And so we have a little riff on that in the film as well with some beautiful footage of the planet. Well, I learned something from that book that had to do with solar eclipses. And I was able to view a solar eclipse whenever it was. Now it feels like it was like six months ago. It was probably about two years ago now. And I was in Illinois and I just was right there for the full solar eclipse. But the unusual circumstance with the moon and its size and its distance from the earth relative to the sun is it in a full solar eclipse, it perfectly covers the sun except for the exterior part, you know, the flame. Oh, the outer glow. Yeah, yeah, yeah. I forget the terms, but you know, it just perfectly covers. So that allows us to see things that we would never see because of the sun, you know, gets in the way. The solar system in deep space. We would never have discovered the Big Bang, for example, without some of these localized coincidences. And so the very strange convergence that Jay and Guillermo discuss is that not only do you have all this fine-tuning to make life possible, but the same set of fine-tuning parameters that make life possible make this completely separate phenomenon possible, which is our ability to make fundamental discoveries about the universe. So they argue that you have this kind of convergence that suggests that life is not only designed, but that it was designed for discovery. That a mind behind the universe. Who wants to be known? You know, who wants creation to be known? All that fiery thing is called the corona. Now it just downloaded for me. My bandwidth is not as wide as it used to be, Stephen. I couldn't remember Vesta's slider, but I had two numbers memorized this week while I'm promoting this film. No, you did great. And it's just so sweet when I'm, from a distance, following how the Lord's using you and the great integrity you demonstrate under fire. And that was obvious that time in Design and Its Critics there in that university. That was wonderful. I have very vivid images of you engaging a respected critic that was less respectful of you than you were of him. And on that, it was just wonderful. You're such a good example to us of so many things. The virtues of a good ambassador in what we call it knowledge, wisdom, character. Knowledge and accurately informed mind, which obviously you do in your field. Wisdom and artful method, obviously. And the character is an attractive manner. And that's just so wonderful, wonderfully apparent in all that you do, Stephen. So I'm glad to count you as a brother and a friend. And I'm glad to be harnessed together with you in this incredible enterprise of advancing the kingdom. We really appreciate you sharing your platform and your audience with us and giving us the chance to tell them about this resource. We're really excited about it. The book, Return of the God Hypothesis, was three and a half years in the writing. The film was five years in the making. Oh, yeah. So I'm here in D.C. right now and kind of committed to leaving it all on the court as far as getting the message out any way we can just to let people know about it. People who have seen the film have been, I think, really touched by it and really affected by it. And we want especially young people who have only heard the other story of reality and their atheistic science professors to hear the case for God and give it a fair shot. The story of everything dot film. The story of everything dot film. That's where you get all the skinny on it. Stephen, what a treat to talk with you today. Thanks for your time. You too, Greg.
